headings.text: The Retrofit Audit That Never Came
Thirteen Years After, the Red Zone Holds
Brick by Brick, the 2015 Lessons Forgotten
Permafrost, and the Foundation That Followed It Down
A Million Buildings, A Deadline of Whispers
On the Hillside, an Unmapped Census
Unreinforced Masonry, and a 50-Year Grace Period
When the Capital Sinks Faster Than It Shakes

paragraphs.text: Eleven weeks, nine cities, one circumnavigation of the Pacific Rim. Correspondents file from the rubble lines, the retrofit yards, and the kitchen tables where reconstruction stalls.

In Sumida ward, an eight-story office block built in 1979 sits two doors down from its retrofitted twin. The owners of the unreinforced building were sent a notice in 2003. Twenty-one years on, no inspector has returned. "We comply with what is enforced," the building manager says, "and what is enforced is the rent."

The eastern suburbs were cleared in 2011. Today, lawnmowers move across the empty plots in regular Friday formation — a maintenance contract the city cannot legally cancel. Former residents return on weekends to walk what used to be streets. "I still know which letterbox was mine," says one. "I just can't tell you why."

The reconstruction grant program closed in 2022 with 14% of approved homes still unbuilt. In Bhaktapur, mason cooperatives that trained in confined masonry now compete with cheaper unreinforced contractors. "The engineers come on Tuesday," one homeowner explains. "On Wednesday, the cheaper builder comes back."

A 1964-era ranch house in the Government Hill neighborhood has settled 47 centimeters in five years. The owners learned only when their stove tilted. Beneath the slab, the active layer has deepened by half a meter since 2018. The city's seismic code does not, and cannot, address what is melting underneath.

Urban transformation officials concede that of 1.2 million buildings flagged as high-risk in 2012, fewer than 230,000 have been replaced or strengthened. The remaining stock sits across the North Anatolian fault's projected slip-line. "We have the law," a ministry engineer says, off the record. "We do not have the will."

In the informal settlements ringing the capital, an estimated 800,000 households live on slopes the geological service classifies as Category IV — the highest liquefaction hazard. None appear on the municipal retrofit register, because none possess the title deeds the registry requires.

After the April 3 quake, evacuation times in the Da'an district averaged 4 minutes 18 seconds — well inside protocol. The architecture is two decades younger than Tokyo's median, the codes stricter, the enforcement publicized monthly. Survivor accounts converge on a single word: rehearsed.

The Pearl District contains 1,640 unreinforced masonry buildings. A 2019 ordinance requiring placards was rescinded after a property-owners' lawsuit. The Cascadia subduction zone, meanwhile, has not moved noticeably since 1700. Seismologists put the next slip somewhere in the next century. The buildings put it lower.

North Jakarta has subsided 2.4 meters since 1990. The new capital at Nusantara takes shape 1,400 km away, but 10 million people remain on the alluvial plain. Seismic risk here competes with — and is amplified by — a foundation that loses ground every monsoon.

The data desk gives you the index score. The investigation gives you the architecture of failure. Field Notes gives you the kitchen table where reconstruction stalled, the inspector who never returned, the deed that was never issued.
Reading them together is the point. Reading any one alone is a partial map of a moving ground.

visibleText: Meridian Fault Dispatch Seismic risk · Infrastructure · Climate displacement Vol. VII · No. 34 · October 2024 Front Investigation Data Desk Field Notes Archive Dispatches · Autumn 2024 Field Notes Eleven weeks, nine cities, one circumnavigation of the Pacific Rim. Correspondents file from the rubble lines, the retrofit yards, and the kitchen tables where reconstruction stalls. A Note on the Fieldwork "Between July 14 and September 28, four correspondents covered 38,400 kilometers across nine Pacific Rim cities. Each spent a minimum of four working days on site, conducting interviews with municipal seismologists, retrofit contractors, displaced households, and the engineers who write the codes nobody enforces. The excerpts below distill thousands of pages of notebooks; full dispatches — with sources, GPS waypoints, and uncut transcripts — are available in the archive." — H. "We comply with what is enforced," the building manager says, "and what is enforced is the rent." 2,840 words Filed Jul 19 M. Vertical excursions correspond to felt events of M≥3.5 recorded within 200km of each correspondent's position. Source: USGS, JMA, GNS, AFAD. From the Editor Why Field Notes The data desk gives you the index score. The investigation gives you the architecture of failure. Field Notes gives you the kitchen table where reconstruction stalled, the inspector who never returned, the deed that was never issued. Reading them together is the point. Reading any one alone is a partial map of a moving ground. — H. Vandermeer Glossary Liquefaction The temporary loss of strength in saturated, loose granular soils during ground shaking. Affected ground behaves as a viscous fluid. Retrofit Rate Share of pre-code buildings within a jurisdiction that have been seismically upgraded to current standard. URM Unreinforced Masonry. Brick, stone, or block construction without internal steel reinforcement; among the deadliest stock in moderate-to-strong shaking. Active Layer The seasonally thawing zone above permafrost. Its deepening compromises foundations engineered for a fixed boundary. Subduction Slip The sudden release of accumulated strain at a plate boundary where one plate descends beneath another. Category IV Hazard Highest liquefaction susceptibility class on the Peruvian geological service's four-tier scale.
